one_line_takeaway: AbnorDetector shows that jailbreak, hallucination, and backdoor behaviors leave detectable fingerprints in a few critical Attention/MLP layers of an LLM, so a lightweight MLP over neuron-activation features (NAS/ANE) can catch them in real time with ~5% overhead and >90% accuracy on most threats, though it needs white-box internals, per-threat labeled training data, and struggles on subtle hallucinations.
key_finding: Across four models AbnorDetector reports average detection accuracies of 97.43% (jailbreak), 82.69% (hallucination), and 91.96% (backdoor), consistently beating the task-specific SOTA baselines while adding only ~5% inference overhead at (alpha,beta)=(0.25,0.25) and as little as 1.2-1.5% on the 70B model. MLP-layer activations are more discriminative than attention layers (jailbreak: 99.07% MLP-only vs 96.33% attn-only on Llama-2). It also generalizes to unseen jailbreak datasets (MASTERKEY, WildJailbreak) at >90% for most model/config pairs, supporting the claim that it captures intrinsic activation signatures rather than surface templates.
core_contribution: The paper introduces AbnorDetector, a general real-time framework for detecting three classes of abnormal LLM behavior (jailbreak attacks, hallucinations, and backdoor triggers) via 'Hidden State Forensics' (HSF): rather than analyzing generated text post-hoc, it inspects layer-specific activation patterns of the LLM's internal hidden states. Its own novel components are: (1) a critical-layer analysis step that ranks Attention and MLP sublayers by how strongly their normal-vs-abnormal feature vectors diverge (lowest cosine similarity = most discriminative), selecting the top alpha*L attention and beta*L MLP layers; (2) two activation features it defines and names, Neuron Activation Score (NAS, activation intensity of the final input token's hidden state) and Active Neuron Engagement (ANE, count of neurons whose activation exceeds a threshold theta, i.e. breadth of participation); and (3) a lightweight five-layer MLP binary classifier trained separately per threat type on these features. Two configurations are proposed: AbnorDetector-Lite (ANE features, extremely small feature set) and AbnorDetector-Full (NAS features, higher accuracy). NAS/ANE are adapted from prior DL-testing coverage metrics (NC, TKNC, TKNP) cited as [14]-[16]; the paper's contribution is applying and formalizing them as forensic signals for LLM anomaly detection, not inventing coverage metrics themselves.
threat_model: The paper's own Section II-B threat model: attackers are malicious users of an LLM service who aim to trigger abnormal behavior to compromise security/credibility. Baseline capability is black-box access to the inference API (submit queries, observe outputs); in jailbreak and backdoor scenarios attackers may additionally have partial or full knowledge of model architecture, parameters, or training data, plus resources for LLM inference and fine-tuning smaller models. Per-threat capabilities: jailbreak via low-cost templates (role-play) or costlier gradient-based adversarial suffixes; backdoor via training-data/supply-chain poisoning to implant triggers (high cost); hallucination arising naturally from knowledge gaps or intentionally induced via deceptive context (low cost). AbnorDetector is the DEFENDER'S tool: it assumes the defender has white-box access to the model's hidden states (via forward-pass hooks) and access to labeled samples of each known abnormal behavior to train dedicated classifiers (a supervised assumption the paper states explicitly). The discussion acknowledges an adaptive adversary could try to mimic normal activation distributions to evade detection, but argues this imposes a severe optimization constraint.
limitations: (1) Hallucination detection is the weak point: on Truthful-QA, AbnorDetector-Lite is near chance (~47-55%) and Full only reaches ~68%, because subtle question-answer conflicts produce weak activation divergence; strong performance depends on pronounced normal/abnormal separation and on knowledge-augmented datasets (HaluEval-QA, Drowzee, SciQ). (2) It is supervised and per-threat: separate classifiers must be trained for jailbreak, hallucination, and backdoor because feature distributions differ, requiring labeled abnormal samples (semi-/unsupervised extensions are only sketched as future work). (3) Requires white-box access to hidden states via hooks, so it is not applicable to closed API-only models. (4) Only validated on Llama and Gemma families; the authors note transferring to substantially different architectures needs re-doing critical-layer selection and re-tuning theta, with no automated calibration yet. (5) Provides only macro layer-level localization, lacking microscopic causal/mechanistic interpretation. (6) Adaptive evasion by mimicking normal activations is acknowledged but not empirically tested. (7) Hallucination detection still needs the generated answer, so it is not purely pre-generation like jailbreak/backdoor detection.

datasets_used: Alpaca-GPT4 (normal jailbreak-baseline queries, 52K QA pairs; 400 sampled)
quantitative_results: Average detection accuracy: jailbreak 97.43%, hallucination 82.69%, backdoor 91.96% (across four models, per Conclusion/Introduction)
AbnorDetector-Lite (alpha=beta=0.5) jailbreak average accuracy 95.27%-98.93% across the four models
GradSafe (jailbreak baseline) average accuracy only 65.8% over five datasets (82.25% excluding undetectable LAA)
Hallucination: AbnorDetector-Full (0.25,0.25) average accuracy 88.17%-91.88% across models; Truthful-QA remains weak at ~67.96% (Full) and near-chance for Lite

Backdoor: AbnorDetector-Lite (0.5,0.5) average accuracy 99.92% / 87.67% / 99.67% / 80.59% on Llama-2-7b / Llama-3.1-8B / Llama-3.1-70B / Gemma; AbnorDetector-Full reaches full (100%) detection in test set
ONION (backdoor baseline) trigger-elimination success only 56.20% / 70.40% / 87.00% at ts=75/50/25, and removes ~22% of prompt words at ts=25
Ablation on Llama-2: MLP-only jailbreak accuracy 99.07% vs attention-only 96.33% vs combined 98.93%

Latency: classifier inference ~0.04s (Lite) and <0.2s (Full) per batch; relative overhead ~5% at (0.25,0.25), dropping to 1.2-1.5% on Llama-3.1-70B; peak GPU memory 26.39GB (Llama-2-7b) to 140.72GB (Llama-3.1-70B)
Generalization: MASTERKEY and WildJailbreak >90% accuracy for most model/config pairs (e.g. Full reaches 100% on 8B/70B/Gemma for both)
RSA: jailbreak inter-class distance 0.333+/-0.053 vs intra-class 0.167+/-0.055
theta robustness: under +/-25% variation, top-50% critical-layer ranking overlap stays above 80%
baselines_compared: GradSafe (jailbreak detection via safety-critical gradient analysis)
